## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"Do you think you have endometres of denomyosis?"

video_id: 7650491657414085910
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7650491657414085910

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
Do you think you have endometres of denomyosis? Have you been told, but you don't really understand or you just don't know where to go next for help? Then we have developed a clinic which is available online which will help you get to the next step and to understand how your symptoms are affecting you. What are the options of your management whether it be medical or surgical or any other techniques such as physiotherapy? You'll come up with a plan to help make you feel better to help you understand your symptoms better and to give you options of how best to manage you. Which suits you lifestyle, and includes your quality of life and your activities of daily life.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Did you know you can see an endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist (CNS) online? Meet Liz Bruen, one of our CNS specialists with over 20 years of experience in endometriosis care. Book your appointment with her, link in bio.

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"Work like symptoms."

video_id: 7650472969264844054
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7650472969264844054

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
Work like symptoms. Work out what's typical, what isn't sick of. Work it out. Go to one of the end of the UK, fantastic. There's some really good resources. You'll and have a look at work like symptoms. Look at yourself and say, okay, what's happening? What's happening to me? And then go to somebody and say, but this is what's happening to me. Can you help? But just keep that clean up and on the door. Don't be scared. There are a lot of them out there who want help. I'm going to help. I'm going to help. I'm going to help.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

If you are hitting a wall getting a diagnosis for endometriosis, this one is for you. The best thing you can do is map out your symptoms. What is cyclical, what is not. Details like:  this stops me from going to work or school or I'm passing out on day 1. Even if you think it's not important, write it down. And if you need help figuring out who to see privately or book a session with Liz - link in bio.

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"If you're needing to use really strong pain killers, get help."

video_id: 7650479358867492118
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7650479358867492118

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
If you're needing to use really strong pain killers, get help. If you're passing out on day one, in your end school, then your friends and your teachers should be noticing it and say, this ain't normal. I never passed out on my period ever, but I know lots of people who do. So we need people going into schools and educating. Because this is not something you get 18 and 21. This can start when you are 11 and 12. They will impact on your education, it will impact on your ability to socialize, you really speak fans, it will get you labeled. And the girls don't want to be labeled. They don't want to be distanced in anybody else. So these are all the other factors, which are grown in there as well.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Painful periods are often one of the first and most well-known symptoms of endometriosis. But it doesn't stop there. It will progress. This is not just for those already diagnosed with endo, this is for parents, teachers and classmates too.  Let's spread the gwarnes about Endometriosis. Liz Bruen was the first endometriosis nurse in Wales.  She has now moved to a private virtual space, so if you want to book a consultation with her, go to the link in our bio.

- Saves: 264
- Shares: 93

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"I would be very surprised to know what you said, no."

video_id: 7650466482173791510
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7650466482173791510

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
I would be very surprised to know what you said, no. All that the Scott Pink is a procedure for facilities will have the ability to record and take pictures. Just not only reasonable to respect, but to expect to be able to see a copy of them. I think sort of as all conversations are kept right. And, you know, understanding on both sides, I don't see it ever be a problem at all. In fact, every professional I know would be more than keen to discuss it with you so that you understand. I'm a great believer in photos, I often get told off. I'll do more photos than I've already, but I think it's so important because it helps you visualize as the person who's directly affected by it, where the disease is and how it's affecting you and why you may be having the symptoms that you have. Which are all very individual to each person.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Always ask for your surgical photos after a laparoscopy.  Always. Those images are your medical records and you are entitled to them, but most people don't know to ask.  If your results came back clear but something still doesn't feel right, a specialist can review those photos and often spot what was missed.  Endometriosis doesn't always look the way surgeons expect, and subtle lesions are easily overlooked without the right training.  Your photos could be the difference between years more of fighting, and finally getting answers.  Liz covers exactly this in her virtual clinic - link in bio to book.

================================================================================
VIDEO 5 / 39 — id 7650467196187036950
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)
- Post date (UTC): 2026-06-12
- Duration: 55s
- Views: 7,857
- Likes: 315
- Comments: 6
- Saves: 320
- Shares: 64

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"Are you going to look at my diaphragm?"

video_id: 7650467196187036950
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7650467196187036950

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
Are you going to look at my diaphragm? Are you going to look at my appendix? Are you going to look at my bowel on the top to the bottom? Are you going to check the pouch of Douglas? Are you going to look at both ovaries above the middle? Are you going to look at the space between the uterus and the pouch of Douglas? And when you're looking at the pouch of Douglas, which is the base of the pelvis, are you going to elevate that you can see the skin stretched out and the abdomen stretched out. So just to see if there's any often and uterus may hide, particularly the vagina will hide within that space. And that can be asked more for a reasonably, it's not an unreasonable request on your behalf to know. Also, are you going to have pictures or videos or both that I can then access and potentially have a copy for my own methods to it? And I think that's a perfectly reasonable discussion to have with your medical professional.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Before your laparoscopy, there are questions you should be asking your surgeon and most people don't know what they are.  Liz covers everything, including what your surgeon should be looking at and why asking for photos or a video afterwards is completely reasonable.  Want more personalised advice?  Liz runs a virtual clinic and you can book a session via the link in our bio.

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"the first response and it still is and it's just so sad is his periods get used to it."

video_id: 7650465917700279574
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7650465917700279574

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
the first response and it still is and it's just so sad is his periods get used to it. So it's simple and complicated. Basically, the majority will start developing issues in their periods, okay? And unfortunately there is a social and clinical belief that, oh, you have to get your experience is rubbish, right? But that's education, okay? So that's one of the issues and then you've got all the other issues of being a teenager, kind of complicated things, and also endometres can present as IBS because they get bloating. The crucial factor that you need to know when you're going to see somebody is to say, this happens every month, but add that complex, that you can have patients who have the increase in symptoms, which happen every month, but then they'll have significant pain for the rest of the month as well. And that's still in, because there's different types, there's different types of endometres, it affects people differently. Nobody will present the same way. It's really good to look at symptoms, symptoms, you know? So you see, you just keep a record of your own tools and take that with you. The problem is is that when you go to see your GP, you'll often have eight minutes or less to tell them what the problem is. If you're a teenager, you will find difficult possibility to communicate it. If you're an adult and listening a lot of years, you're exasperated, there's that break in communication. Also, there's not enough education about it. It is better. There are guidelines out there, but people need to have awareness of what it possibly could be. There are easy questions which will mark you towards endometrances, but you need to ask them. Are you need to listen to the answers? And that's the important thing.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

9 years. That's how long it takes, on average, to get an endometriosis diagnosis in the UK. And it's not just the system. Awareness, dismissed symptoms, pain that gets written off as just a bad period, not knowing what's normal - it all adds up Liz Bruen, Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist with 20 years of experience, breaks down why the delay happens and what you can actually do about it. If you've been waiting, going in circles, or just need someone who gets it.

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"This is purely diagnostic."

video_id: 7650429101584469270
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7650429101584469270

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
This is purely diagnostic. I would suggest that everybody should give photos and should give a detailed analysis of what they have observed during the procedure to the patient to help them understand whether or not it was they found nothing abnormal. You still need photos to show it. I think the photos, as I said earlier, are a crucial part of the care package when you do not have a specific surgery. Okay, because it gives you something to relate to. And some people won't have it though when they have pain, but they won't have endometreuses or maybe we're operating on you a bit too young and we still can see it. Okay, so I would ask for a second opinion if that was a case, because you'd like to pure diagnose it. And if it was 30 minutes for the whole procedure, it was very quick.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

A 30-minute laparoscopy that found nothing doesn't mean nothing is there.  A diagnostic laparoscopy is only as good as the person performing it, and not every surgeon is trained to spot every form of endometriosis. Endometriosis nurse Liz breaks down exactly why that happens, and shares something really important that every single person should do after their surgery: ask for your photos. Those images could make all the difference if you ever need a second opinion. Watch before your next appointment.

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"I had my stuff thrown on your own and the train was full and then you know seats and they tried to look to prime up to me and said that he will find me and see if you've got a land on your own."

video_id: 7650157296907062550
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7650157296907062550

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
I had my stuff thrown on your own and the train was full and then you know seats and they tried to look to prime up to me and said that he will find me and see if you've got a land on your own. You're ever shrugling that they can help and they can help find you someone which was really really nice. I do think there's a benefit to it and I think you can also help with confidence because I remember when I first got it I was a conference where I was I don't want to bring no one around where they export. The more I worry it was just like recognising them you know I do have a condition that means I do need help sometimes and that's okay and you know I am awake where they said I need to wear it sometimes so I do believe it can help in many ways especially when you're travelling or you and the airport things like that like I do think it's a good thing.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Did you know that if you have endometriosis, you're eligible for a Hidden Disabilities Sunflower lanyard? @Laura | Women’s Health Creator shares her experience of wearing one and how it's made a real difference when travelling and on days when a flare-up hits. Because nobody should have to suffer in silence or feel too embarrassed to ask for help. The lanyard is free and there are no eligibility requirements, you simply request one. If you think it could help you, head to hdsunflower.com/uk to find out more.

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"I would say Lester G, Lester G is always, I did a study a year ago, I was asked to do a presentation about my clinic at BSG and called, and I did it and I thought it was boring."

video_id: 7649808436883967254
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7649808436883967254

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
I would say Lester G, Lester G is always, I did a study a year ago, I was asked to do a presentation about my clinic at BSG and called, and I did it and I thought it was boring. So I've been taxed in 100 patients with 10 questions, 95% had the teeth, 70% had the disperointing of painful intercourse, 80% had the painful periods. That, and that again, is an issue because the teeth could be many things, and be that you'd be working too hard, it can be that you're not eating well, I've been dieting, you're drinking too much alcohol, it could be many, many things. And if the teeth would believe it's due to an ongoing inflammatory process in the body, which is a little ticking, it's like a drip in your roof. You know, which is going to make a seeding fall, but you don't really know it's there until the seeding falls down, that's what it is.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

The pain is real, it's debilitating, and it deserves to be talked about. But there's another symptom that affects more than half of people with endo, one that gets dismissed, overlooked, and rarely shows up in the conversation. It's not straightforward, it's not the same for everyone, and understanding it could change the way you talk to your doctor.

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)

- Saves: 4
- Shares: 1

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"I just got on the pill when I was a laughing to kind of tackle that that went on until 15 and the word and do it truces was then brought up on iron tablets, calories and so much blood, like I'd be having to change all the time and bed sheets and matt"

video_id: 7647939769464278275
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7647939769464278275

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
I just got on the pill when I was a laughing to kind of tackle that that went on until 15 and the word and do it truces was then brought up on iron tablets, calories and so much blood, like I'd be having to change all the time and bed sheets and mattresses and no one kind of thought it was like, oh, it's just heavy pain for periods. You know, when you've had a few little settle, it just takes a few years and that went on until 15 and the word and do it truces was then brought up for four long years. I remember just thinking, I used to dread it and then I was never one of them people because I first got on the pill when I was a laughing to kind of tackle that and that then sent my body everywhere like I could never be and even now I could never track my period so you know maybe I'd be bleeding heavily for three weeks and then it would stop for a couple days and then it'd come back and then it would stop and I was so out of sync with my body which then made it really hard because then I was on the pill but my symptoms still remained and I remember people just thinking, you know, try a different pill, try different pain killers, you know, if you had a bath, when is this your fifth period like weight so you experienced it for a few years and it was just constant every excuse not to find out what was wrong with me and it made it really really hard.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

We’ll put you on the pill, that will sort you out. This is just your first period, wait until it settles. Ayla was only 11 years old when her symptoms started. Despite experiencing severe pain, it took 4 years of being dismissed before she finally received her endometriosis diagnosis. This is what endometriosis looks like in teens.  It’s not just a bad period, and it doesn’t just affect adults.

VIDEO 11 / 39 — id 7647574808162487574
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)
- Post date (UTC): 2026-06-04
- Duration: 141s
- Views: 937
- Likes: 27
- Comments: 0
- Saves: 3
- Shares: 0

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"It was actually the day before I started high school."

video_id: 7647574808162487574
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7647574808162487574

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
It was actually the day before I started high school. I got my period, so that was just completely not a good time at all. And it was actually the first day I was in PE. Normally I was doing track, I was doing dance and I'd done that since two years old, never had any issues. And then randomly on my first day we would do a PE and I fainted. I remember being in such debilitating pain. I got sent to school nurse at the time. I just remember looking down and I was bleeding everywhere and I just remember thinking like why am I soaking through pads and having to change my pads, you know, every ten minutes but at the time because it was the first day of my period I'd never ever experienced this before. I just thought oh you know maybe it's normal and you know I remember telling the teachers like oh like I need to get changed or I really don't feel well and they were like oh no you know you'll just get used to it and I was like but I'm in like severe pain and I felt like I couldn't really ask anyone else because they either hadn't gotten their period yet or if they had you know it wasn't any different to normal day life. But I remember thinking like paracetamol wasn't helping, I was passing out, I was dizzy, I was having to change my pad every ten minutes and you know going to school then you know like from primary school I was in every day, my attendance was always fine and then at high school it just dipped because at every time I'd get my period which was never regular either, it would be all over the place and I either would be sent home from school and be in the school nurse's office, I couldn't go to dance lessons and that was really unheard of from me. So that was when me myself and my family were like this isn't right so then that was when I went to the GP and kind of said I'm really unwell around my periods like nobody else seems to either be hospitalised or bedbound by their period like what's going on and I think the first few visits it was like oh you know you'll just get used to it you know it's because it's your first period you know and I just thought oh I'd go for medical help and everyone tells me you know it's fine that you'll get used to it and I definitely believe that for quite some time but it just got worse and worse and yeah.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

We all have bad periods, you just have to get used to it. Ayla first started experiencing symptoms at 11 years old. It took years before anyone: took her seriously. This is what endo looks like in teenagers and why we need to do better

VIDEO 12 / 39 — id 7647091030986165526
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)
- Post date (UTC): 2026-06-03
- Duration: 95s
- Views: 2,241
- Likes: 39
- Comments: 4
- Saves: 13
- Shares: 1

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"MRI is good in studies it comes with sort of a relatively equal comparison to transvaginal scan the benefit of transvaginal scan is it's cheaper and it's more accessible however it does depend on the skill of the person using it like with everything "

video_id: 7647091030986165526
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7647091030986165526

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
MRI is good in studies it comes with sort of a relatively equal comparison to transvaginal scan the benefit of transvaginal scan is it's cheaper and it's more accessible however it does depend on the skill of the person using it like with everything okay so even more so than MRI MRI is about the interpretation of the result transvaginal scan is about how you use the probe and then interpret the results that you get MRI is very effective in some hospitals it's what's available you know it that's people's practice and they will tend to have radiologists who are extremely efficient and expert analyzing MRI result MRI is great for assessing endometriosis affecting above the rectal sigmoid so that's up to the large bowel the cecum the appendix small bowel going upwards it can be very effective in the pouch of Douglas however in my experience I find that sometimes there is not a correlation between the laparoscopic findings and the MRI findings often the laparoscopic findings which is the gold standard is more indicates worse thing so that's one of the issues with MRI is sometimes it doesn't correlate with laparoscopic findings and when you're doing your procedure sometimes not always but sometimes you may have to do a two stage procedure because it hasn't given you enough information and in fact you do need to do either further investigations or further procedure with a colorectal surgeon

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

A negative MRI does NOT mean you're in the clear! MRI is powerful, especially for endometriosis affecting the bowel, appendix, or pouch of Douglas, but it doesn't always correlate with what's actually found during surgery. Sometimes a two-stage procedure is needed simply because imaging didn't capture the full picture. Your symptoms are valid even when your scan says otherwise.

================================================================================
VIDEO 13 / 39 — id 7645351638957313302
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)
- Post date (UTC): 2026-05-29
- Duration: 65s
- Views: 1,024
- Likes: 17
- Comments: 3
- Saves: 4
- Shares: 3

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"Endo events is basically a community-led advocacy-based project essentially so it's a complete non-profit organisation which is set up essentially to help people with chronic illnesses in particular people with endocrosis and basically this come abou"

video_id: 7645351638957313302
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7645351638957313302

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
Endo events is basically a community-led advocacy-based project essentially so it's a complete non-profit organisation which is set up essentially to help people with chronic illnesses in particular people with endocrosis and basically this come about because I was struggling with my own condition and I had a very lovely and big community on TikTok and everyone was always saying and was always talking about potentially meeting up or doing something so one day I just made a video and I just said I'm thinking about doing an event would anyone turn up would anyone call and it was like yeah so I did it I did the first event people turned up and then everyone was just like the response from that first event was insane so it really like I remember sitting there thinking well I've got to carry on with this like there's no way I can just do one event and say see you later like people I need to continue this like people need this people want this so I just continued and then obviously it just gets bigger and bigger every event it gets better brands want to get involved organisations want to get involved you know it's a place for people to connect to get education through resources

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

@Laura | Women’s Health Creator struggled with her own endometriosis symptoms and found herself building a community around it on TikTok. Now there are actual events. @Your Endo Events is a community project that grew from that exact moment, and if you're free on the 13th of June, the first ever London event is happening. Worth checking out 💛

VIDEO 14 / 39 — id 7644863912545881366

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)

- Duration: 20s
- Views: 17,500
- Likes: 1,072
- Comments: 27
- Saves: 253
- Shares: 88

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"The benefits of MRI depends on who's reading it."

video_id: 7644863912545881366
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7644863912545881366

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
The benefits of MRI depends on who's reading it. If you've got it, the radiologist needs to have an interest in it. If you're having an MRI, it must be with a radiologist who has an understanding of endometriosis and knows what they're looking for. Otherwise, they'll miss it. Same when it comes to surgery.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Your MRI is only as useful as the person reading it. For endo, that matters more than most people reglize.  #endometriosis #endoawareness

VIDEO 15 / 39 — id 7644580086208859414
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)
- Post date (UTC): 2026-05-27
- Duration: 79s
- Views: 806
- Likes: 32
- Comments: 0
- Saves: 2
- Shares: 3

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"I had a really good job."

video_id: 7644580086208859414
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7644580086208859414

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
I had a really good job. I went to university. I was doing all of those things but when I got diagnosed, I was spending meter OCS and my health started getting worse. I found it really hard to navigate a normal job. For me it was something that I just fell into. I started sharing an experience I had and then it kind of just went from there. I was really enjoying what I was doing in the online space and I was helping people and I started doing live streams and we started doing like events and then I started working with brands and it was just honestly like something that I didn't even know was possible. Like I didn't even know that was an option for me to do. When I kind of was really struggling and I was struggling to actually work and do the job that I was doing, I was just like I wonder where I can make my own job. I'm wondering if I can do this and I could do what I love to do and help people all the time as well as like be able to be really creative and do what I like to do. So I kind of just took a risk really. I just took a risk and I was like I'm just gonna do it. Although being a freelancer is hard of course it is but it allows me to be more flexible. So you know I don't need to ask my boss leave for appointments or if I'm in a flare-up I don't have to feel guilty for going home and you know all that kind of stuff. I don't have to leave the office till I've come on period. So it's like it's a lot much better to manage and if you really truly want to do it I think anyone can do it. Like I think anyone can do anything I want if they put the body to it.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Endometriosis takes. It takes your time, your energy, and life as you knew it. But it doesn't have to be the end of the story. @Laura | Women’s Health Creator is living proof that struggle can be transformed into something powerful. She turied her experience with endometriosis into advocacy, content creation, and a nonprofit events, building a community that helps countless women navigate the same road she once walked alone. You are not alone and you don't have to stay that way. Check out @Your Endo Events - a London meetup is coming soon.

================================================================================

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"Do you have questions about endometriosis or adenomyosis?"

video_id: 7644218706028956950
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7644218706028956950

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
Do you have questions about endometriosis or adenomyosis? Do you think you have endometriosis or adenomyosis? Have you been told but you don't really understand or you just don't know where to go next for help? Then we have developed a clinic which is available online which will help you get to the next step and to understand how your symptoms are affecting you. What are the options of your management whether it be medical or surgical or any other techniques such as physiotherapy? Through this clinic, you will be assessed, you will be asked about your pain levels, what investigations have you had done? Are you feeling tired all the time? And then after that, we'll come up with a plan to help make you feel better, to help you understand your symptoms better and to give you options of how best to manage you which suits your lifestyle and improves your quality of life and your activities of daily living.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"whatever your experience in right to don't document it."

video_id: 7643867792634154262
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7643867792634154262

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
whatever your experience in right to don't document it. I am Laura and I am a women's health advocate and contact creator and I'm also a founder of your adult events which is a community-based project for people, struggling with chronic illnesses and endometrosis. So whenever I was going there was always asking for period love. There was always asking for like symptom love, bell love, everything. Anything that I experience I started writing to do. I also started writing down when I experienced it and what I was doing. Was it because I ate something? Was I during the period? Was it because I did exercise? Like whatever was going on, I documented it and I also documented how it affected me on a daily basis. So when I went into my appointments, I had a clear plan and a clear structure of a bright day and of all my symptoms and how it affected me. And I find explaining to a specialist or a GP of how your symptoms debilitate your effective really helps you that advocate because it's showing them and demonstrating them while you're struggling and while you need them to do something. So it's a lot better than just saying, I'm tired. You can say, I am that fatigued that I physically come and get up in the afternoon. You know, something like that. That's why I always say the mind-free things is right during the symptoms, document everything, take someone with you to appointments because it absolutely does help advocate for you and also write down a couple of questions that you want answered before you leave there. So whether that's what are the next steps what treatment plan I'm going to have and we're going to have any scans, what happens if like friends if you're having a lap? Ask the question, what happens if you don't find anything? Because you need to know what happens next. So it's like little things like that, writing everything down document and everything and taking it is always going to be the best way.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Do you document your endo symptoms? @Laura | Women’s Health Creator learned that writing everything down - bowel changes, pain levels, how her cycle affected her day - gave her the clarity and confidence to advocate for herself with every GP and specialist she saw. Your symptoms deserve to be taken seriously. Documenting them is how you make sure they are. Need help organising your notes before an appointment? We can help! Chat to us on WhatsApp.

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
I asked I, I, to tell me what I should say to my GP to get referred to an antimiotross specialist. And that's what I did. When I was diagnosed with antimiotrosses, I was then discharged and there was just like, if you want to have a baby, you need to have body after surgery, come back when you want to get pregnant. All this kind of stuff. After my surgery, my pain had come back and it come back quite severely and I started having over issues that I didn't have before my surgeries. I kept going to the doctors and there was just kind of saying to me, you know, you've been diagnosed with endo, we can refave a pain management, but it's a really long way in this. So they just kept throwing different painkillers at me. I just kept going back and then they agreed to do some scans, they agreed to do like MRIs and all that kind of stuff. Obviously, you know that antimiotrosses doesn't always show up on scans and MRIs and it didn't in my case. So what I did was all of the, all of my scans and all my MRIs are copied and painted, it got into chat to you, be tick. And basically, apparently something on my MRI said something about bone antimiotrosses, covering all that kind of stuff. I asked I, I, to tell me what I should say to my GP to get referred to an antimiotross especially. It's something under the specialist. I felt a lot better. Don't, wrong, I, I can be good and bad, like I've got mixed feelings about it, but I do think it can be a good tool. So I, I didn't help me get my diagnosis. What it did was it helped me get a second opinion with an endometrosis specialist.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Al can be a powerful tool when navigating endometriosis, if you know how to use it. @Laura | Women’s Health Creator shared how she used Al to make sense of her medical records, understand her MRI results, and ask the right questions to get referred to a specialist who finally helped her manage her endometriosis symptoms. But you don't have to figure it out alone. We can help you navigate the system, understanding your symptoms and finding the right private specialist for where you live. Just come and talk to us. ## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
You know, to trust your own body, to trust your own intuition, you know, your body better than anyone else. I was struggling with a lot of symptoms when I was a teenager. I was able to tell some periods. I was having, you know, really long periods. I was like being sick, passing out to school, all that kind of stuff. And I knew something wasn't right, but I didn't really know what it was. And no one really else kind of knew or said anything around me. And it wasn't until I was at university where I actually started getting different symptoms, like fatigue, tiredness, pain during sex, bleeding during sex, all that kind of stuff. And I was like, this is definitely something I need to look into. It's definitely not normal. So I didn't actually start really getting into the process of actually trying to get help and all that kind of stuff until I was at university. So I'll probably let the start, like, early 20s. And then I would go in and obviously, again, I was met with the whole, you know, it's just an old period. It's just because you're a woman. And they didn't really take it seriously. There was just, like, silent conception, do this, do that. But it was never really anything that gave me the answers, it gave me anything to work on. So I was always just kind of, like, fogged off and pushed away. Eventually, they agreed to do a, um, Ella Proske P. And then after my life, they told me I hadn't to be process.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

VIDEO 20 / 39 — id 7641554459755089154
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)
- Post date (UTC): 2026-05-19
- Duration: 26s
- Views: 8,374
- Likes: 187
- Comments: 1
- Saves: 135
- Shares: 42

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"Excision, not ablation, because it's ablation only takes the surface, it's like skimming, it's like skimming something."

video_id: 7641554459755089154
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7641554459755089154

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
Excision, not ablation, because it's ablation only takes the surface, it's like skimming, it's like skimming something. If your plaster's gone on the wall, skimming it is not going to make any difference because it's still going to blow, yeah? Same difference. Endometriosis is too deep to skim over the top of it, because it will hide, it will hide in the foals like that.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Excision, not ablation.  Liz Bruen, endometriosis CNS, explains why this matters for your laparoscopy. She runs her own virtual clinic - link in bio.

VIDEO 21 / 39 — id 7641301413062102294
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)
- Post date (UTC): 2026-05-18
- Duration: 43s
- Views: 2,066
- Likes: 31
- Comments: 4
- Saves: 2
- Shares: 0

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"So, MRI, can I have overdiagnosis underdiagnosis andometrosis?"

video_id: 7641301413062102294
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7641301413062102294

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
So, MRI, can I have overdiagnosis underdiagnosis andometrosis? Is this true? Well, I would say MRI is particularly effective for identifying endometrosis, which affects the bowel above the rectal sigmoid junction. Trans vaginal scan is far more effective in our practice at least in assessing everything else in the pelvis. MRI is the only available resource in many places though, for assessing for rectal vaginal endometrosis, and therefore depends on the quality of the assessment and the analysis of the information which is gained through the MRI, and that depends on user interpretation. But we're working on it, there's a lot of education going on, and it's rapidly improving.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

You asked, Liz answered! Can endometriosis be over or under-diagnosed on MRI? Our endo CNS breaks it down.

video_id: 7641128554654256387
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7641128554654256387

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
Last question, one, six, right. So we do get them, fortunately, surgery doesn't always improve pain. And this is a very frank discussion that we have to have with everybody, who's listed for a blackoscopic procedure, whether it be their first one, or their seventh, eighth or ninth one, or whether it even be whether having his stretch to me, okay? It doesn't necessarily mean pain is what we call multimodal, right? Many things cause it, many things improve it, many things make it worse. Not one thing will answer everything, okay? So the surgery for some people may not improve it. You may not have had enough surgery, okay? You may have not have had a proper assessment. So it's worth having, you know, having that discussion after six months, why have I not improved? Should I be looking at hormones, or should do I need to be reviewed by somebody else?

I think the photos, as I said earlier, are a crucial part of the care package when you're doing laposcopic surgery, okay, because it gives you something to relate to. And some people won't have endo when they have pain, but they won't have endometroses, or maybe we're operating on you a bit too young and we still can't see it, okay? So I would ask for a second opinion if that was the case, because you got to pure diagnostic. And if it was 30 minutes for the whole procedure, then it was very quick.

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
And somebody has asked, was there a conclusion that laparoscopic was far less successful than a full open surgery as far the condition worsening versus being better treated down the road? Not in my experience laparoscopic is key whole surgery. Open surgery is a big incision across your kosher tummy. So immediately the healing takes a lot longer. You can't see with laparoscope is like looking through something with 3,000% improvement in your eye exposure whereas a laparotomy which is an open procedure and looking at it with my bare eyes. And I've terrible eyesight. So you can't see it nearly as well. The other beauty of laparoscopic surgery is that you use your instruments to feel the tissue and you can actually feel where they have normal tissue is. Sometimes when you can't even see it you can feel it and it feels quite rough and gritty makes that a tick tick sound when you touch it.

All laposcopic procedure facilities will have the ability to record and take pictures, so it's not unreasonable to expect to be able to see a copy of them. I think so on and all conversations are kept light and you know, understanding on both sides, I don't see it's ever be a problem at all. In fact, every professional I know would be more than keen to discuss it with you so that you understand. I'm a great believer in photos, I often get told off. I'll do more photos than everyone already, but I think it's so important because it helps you visualize as the person who's directly affected by it, where they'd see is and how it's affecting you and why you may be having the symptoms that you have. We show all very individual to each person.

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)

- Views: 1,025

- Comments: 5
- Saves: 14
- Shares: 2

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"Hi, good afternoon everybody."

video_id: 7639345448112672022
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7639345448112672022

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
Hi, good afternoon everybody. It's Lizzie Brewing here again. There's nice to all again. Thank you so many to all of you who have looked at our 360 degree video. It's been an incredible response. We are very very very happy that we are hopefully helping you. There have been some questions raised and I'm just going to address them. So the first question was what point do we ask for this? Well whenever you're talking about surgery, basically, you can just bring it up in the conversation when you're with your consultant or your medical professional and they're discussing with you about the options of care. They will discuss medical and they will discuss surgical. When they're talking about the surgery, they'll say, well, what do you look at? Which areas of my body would end up with the pelvis? Do you look at when you're doing the assessment? And that gives you your avenue into ask for all the things which we stated in the 360 degree. In other words, are you going to look at my diaphragm? Are you going to look at my appendix? Are you going to look at my bowel or on the top to the bottom? Are you going to check the pouch of Douglas? Are you going to look at both ovaries above and below? Are you going to look at the space between the uterus and the womb between the uterus and the pouch of Douglas? And when you're looking at the pouch of Douglas, which is the base of the pelvis, are you going to elevate that so that you can see the skin stretched out, so just to see if there's any often enemy tools that may hide particular to the general, will hide within that space? And that can be asked for very reasonably, it's not an unreasonable request on your behalf to know. Also, are you going to have pictures or video? Or both that I can then access and potentially have a copy for my own records afterwards? And I think that's a perfectly reasonable discussion to have with your medical professional.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

VIDEO 29 / 39 — id 7638541093646191894
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)

- Duration: 50s
- Views: 1,158
- Likes: 49
- Comments: 1
- Saves: 5
- Shares: 1

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"The research that's going on, the fact that I know so many people that give a damn about it, that really want to make a difference, and that people are being empowered to also help themselves."

video_id: 7638541093646191894
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7638541093646191894

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
The research that's going on, the fact that I know so many people that give a damn about it, that really want to make a difference, and that people are being empowered to also help themselves. There is more help out there. There's tools out there. There's ways of getting information. Sometimes you may need guidance towards that information, but the information is there. There are ways to improve it. Unfortunately, it is a condition which may take a while to settle and may always be a problem, but there are ways of adapting so that you can have a good quality of life, maximise your quality of life and your ability to function. That's what I and everyone I know want to do. That's our prime drive, maximise maximum quality of life, maximum ability to function.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

It's not all doom and gloom. Researchers are now describing endometriosis as a "cancer-mimicking" disease. Meaning it shares biological features with cancer in the way it grows, invades tissue, and resists treatment. This isn't just terminology. It's proof that people are actively working to change how this condition is understood and taken seriously. Liz is an Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist based in Wales, offering online consultations. If you want to speak to her, link in bio.

================================================================================
VIDEO 30 / 39 — id 7637168703532027158
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)
- Post date (UTC): 2026-05-07
- Duration: 63s
- Views: 2,612
- Likes: 97
- Comments: 4
- Saves: 21
- Shares: 7

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"I would say lethargy."

video_id: 7637168703532027158
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7637168703532027158

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
I would say lethargy. I did a study years ago. I was asked to do a presentation about my clinic at the BSG in Caulf and I did it and I thought it was boring. So I've texted 100 patients. Guess how many replied? With 10 questions. 100%. 100% replied. 100 women. 95% had fatigue. I gave them all the symptoms. What did they have? 70% had dysparonia, painful intercourse. 80% had painful periods. And that again is an issue because fatigue could be many things. It could be that you've been working too hard. It could be that you're not eating well, been dieting, you're drinking too much alcohol. You know it could be many many things and the fatigue we believe is due to like an ongoing inflammatory process in the body which is a low ticking. It's like a drip in your roof you know which is going to make your ceiling fall but you don't really know it's there until the ceiling falls down. That's what it is.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Endometriosis isn't just about painful periods. Chronic fatigue is one of its most common symptoms, and one of the most dismissed, because fatigue can have so many causes, it's rarely the first thing that points doctors toward endometriosis. If you're exhausted in ways that feel bigger than your cycle, it's worth investigating. Liz is an Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist who offers online consultations. Anyone needing help navigating this, she's your person. Link in bio 🔗

Otherwise they'll miss it. Same when it comes to surgery.

VIDEO 32 / 39 — id 7634274846117104918
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)
- Post date (UTC): 2026-04-29
- Duration: 60s
- Views: 1,106
- Likes: 51
- Comments: 1
- Saves: 4
- Shares: 1

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"There's not enough education about it."

video_id: 7634274846117104918
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7634274846117104918

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
There's not enough education about it. It is better. There are guidelines out there but people need to have awareness of what it possibly could be. There are easy questions which will mark you towards endometriosis but you need to ask them and you need to listen to the answers and that's the important thing. But there are complex symptoms with endometriosis. You get pain on intercourse, you get pain opening your vials, pain opening your bladder but those also happen for other things as well and what happens unfortunately is that people get multiple referrals to many other people and they may even get referrals to gynecology but not get asked that question is this endometriosis. It will then be asked oh it's painful periods well we'll just treat it as you know a period problem and they treat it as a period problem and it may or may not respond but it and that is one of the issues with it is it's very individual and how people respond is extremely individual and the impact of the symptoms is individual but it's simple and complicated.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Why does it take 9 years on average to get an endometriosis diagnosis in the UK? It's a simple question with a complicated answer, and Liz breaks it all down in part two. Liz Bruen is an Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist with 20 years of experience and you can book a virtual consultation with her directly.

VIDEO 33 / 39 — id 7633862545228434710
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)
- Post date (UTC): 2026-04-28
- Duration: 80s
- Views: 1,952
- Likes: 105
- Comments: 0
- Saves: 17
- Shares: 0

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"So it's simple and complicated."

video_id: 7633862545228434710
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7633862545228434710

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
So it's simple and complicated. Basically, the majority will start developing issues in their periods, okay? And unfortunately, there is a social and clinical belief that, oh, you have to get used to your periods. It's rubbish. Right? But that's education. Okay? So that's one of the issues. And then you've got all the other issues of being a teenager, can complicate things. And also, endometriosis can present as IBS because it gets bloating. The crucial factor that you need to know when you're going to see somebody is to say, this happens every month. But add that to the complex that we can have patients who have the increase of symptoms which happen every month. But then they will have significant pain for the rest of the month as well. And that's still ending. Because there's different types of endometriosis. It affects people differently. Nobody will present in the same way. It's really good to look at symptoms. Symptom tools, you know. So you just keep a record of your own tools and take that with you. The problem is that when you go to see your GP, you'll often have eight minutes or less to tell them what the problem is. If you're a teenager, you will find it difficult possibly to communicate it. If you're an adult and it's been going on for years, you're exasperated. So you just don't know, you know. So there's that break in communication, I feel.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

9 years. That's how long it takes, on average, to get an endometriosis diagnosis in the UK. And as much as the system needs to change, it's not the only reason. Awareness, language, being dismissed, not knowing what's "normal".  It all plays a part. Liz Bruen, Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist with 20 years of experience, breaks down why the delay happens, and what you can actually do about it. Liz offers virtual consultations where she'll go through your full symptom history, help you understand your condition, and put together a care plan tailored to you. If you've been waiting, going in circles, or just need someone who actually gets it, her link is in bio.

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"If someone has suspicion that they have endometriosis, but they're kind of hitting the wall What would you tell them now?"

video_id: 7633495707416235286
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7633495707416235286

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
If someone has suspicion that they have endometriosis, but they're kind of hitting the wall What would you tell them now? Like what's the next step or what's something they should they should know? Work out your symptoms. Work out what's typical, what isn't typical. Work it out. Go to one in the end of UK It's fantastic. There's some really good resources. Go and have a look, but work out your symptoms Look at yourself and say, okay, what's happening? What's happening to me and then go to somebody and say this is what's happening to me. Can you help? But just keep on, keep knocking on the doors You know, don't despair. There are a lot of us out there who want to help. Those people who want to help So, or you can come and see me in help and I do virtual clinic as well

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Hitting a wall getting a diagnosis for endometriosis? This one is for you. The best thing you can do is map out your symptoms. What is cyclical, what is not. Details like: this stops me from going to work or school or l'm passing out on day 1. Even if you think it's not important, write it down. And if you need help summarising your notes, or figuring out who to see privately, we have a WhatsApp just for that.  Link in bio.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

You have approximately 8 minutes to tell your GP what's wrong, but how do you get it right? None of us enjoy going to the doctor, and the time pressure makes things worse.  You have one chance to explain your problem properly, otherwise you risk being dismissed. So what makes the difference?  Details. Make notes about your symptoms and be as specific as you can. If you're not sure where to start, we can help. We specialise in supporting patients with endometriosis, so if you're struggling to put together clear notes before your next appointment, just drop us a WhatsApp message. Link in bio.

- Views: 1,423
- Likes: 49
- Comments: 4
- Saves: 10
- Shares: 1

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"If you're needing to use really strong pain killers, get help."

video_id: 7632002231339928854
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7632002231339928854

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
If you're needing to use really strong pain killers, get help. Get help, 100%. If you're passing out on day one and you're in school, then your friends and your teachers should be noticing it and saying, this ain't normal. I never passed out on my period ever, but I know lots of people who do. So we need people going into schools and educating because this is not something you get at 18 and 21. This can start when you are 11 and 12 and it will impact on your education, your ability to socialize, your ability to make friends. It'll get you labeled and the girls don't want to be labeled. They don't want to be different to everybody else. So these are all the other factors which are brought in there as well.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Periods can be painful, but if you need strong painkillers to be able to function? GET HELP! Painful periods are often one of the first and most well-known symptoms of endometriosis. But it doesn't stop there. It will progress. This is not just for those already diagnosed with endo, this is for parents, teachers and classmates too. Let's spread the awarnes about Endometriosis.  Liz Bruen was the first endometriosis nurse in Wales. She has now moved to a private virtual space, so if you want to book a consultation with her, go to the link in our bio.

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)

- Views: 9,982
- Likes: 338
- Comments: 13
- Saves: 183
- Shares: 60

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"Excision, not ablation."

video_id: 7631220659770690818
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7631220659770690818

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
Excision, not ablation. Ablation only takes the surface. It's like skimming something. If you need, if your plaster's gone on the wall, skimming it is not going to make any difference is it, because it's still going to blow. Yeah? Same difference. Endometriosis is too deep to skim over the top of it. It goes too deep. Hi, my name is Liz Bruin. I am an Endometriosis Clinical Specialist. I was the very first Endometriosis nurse since 2006 in Wales.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

Responding to a question from today: Can someone explain why excision, not ablation? Short answer: Surface level isn't enough. Liz Bruen (Endometriosis CNS & Surgical Assistant) explains that ablation is just "skimming." Treating what’s on the surface is only half the battle.  If you want to understand why endometriosis symptoms often "re-surface" after surgery, listen to this.

================================================================================
VIDEO 38 / 39 — id 7630900114982210838
================================================================================

## Analytics (public TikTok counts)
- Post date (UTC): 2026-04-20
- Duration: 67s
- Views: 325,700
- Likes: 26,100
- Comments: 553
- Saves: 17,780
- Shares: 4,093

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"If you have a laparoscopy of somebody who doesn't know what they're looking for, they'll miss it."

video_id: 7630900114982210838
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7630900114982210838

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
If you have a laparoscopy of somebody who doesn't know what they're looking for, they'll miss it. You need to do what we call a 360 degree assessment, and I think everyone should ask for this. I tell every patient, wherever you're going to have surgery with, say, I want to have a 360 degree assessment, I want to have pictures to show that you've looked at my diaphragm, checked whether I've got thoracic endo, checked all my bowels, checked my appendix, checked my ovaries, checked my uterus to bladder, and then uterus to pouch of Douglas, checked the bowel, moved the bowel around, checked to see whether I've got any hiding, because if you have rect vaginal endo, which is endometriosis, sitting in the pouch of Douglas. So imagine, imagine that's the pouch of Douglas. You can see that. Okay, so imagine if I elevate that like that, okay, and I had a spot in there. You can't see it when it's like that. When I open it up like that, you can see it, and that's what people need to do. You need to elevate the pouch of Douglas, so that's the first thing, and then excision, not ablation.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

This is for anyone heading into laparoscopic surgery for endo, or those currently on the waiting list. We recently had the chance to chat with Liz Bruen, a private Endometriosis Clinical Nurse  Specialist in Wales. What she shared about the laparoscopy process was absolutely mind-blowing. If you would like to speak to Liz yourself, you can find prices, availability and book a zoom appointment with her directly by visiting the link in our bio!

## Hook (first spoken sentence)
"See, this is the good news of social media."

video_id: 7629626326927805718
url: https://www.tiktok.com/@docmap/video/7629626326927805718

## Spoken transcript (Whisper automatic speech recognition, English)
See, this is the good news of social media. Yeah, it's not all doom and gloom. You have a community of almost 30,000 people. How does that feel? Terrifying. Absolutely terrifying. No, I love it. It can be really scary. I'll be honest with you, like it really can. I love sharing my story and my opinions. But obviously, when you're on social media, not everyone would agree with you. People aren't afraid to say their true feelings because, you know, they can hide behind a screen. But I'm so lucky that the community that I've gone on Instagram are absolutely fantastic. Like I literally love every single one of them. I just wish I had the time to sit down and talk to them all individually. So as terrifying as it is, I absolutely love it.

## TikTok title and description (verbatim from post metadata)

From a heartbreaking diagnosis to a community of 30k people (from this week!). But the numbers can be, to an extent, terrifying, as Amy has admitted. The sense of not being alone is what makes the endo community so strong and truly supportive. Essentially, the community around endo is what helps many to cope mentally. How did the endo community help you? @growwithendo
